target: data/wins.json WIN-068 — replacement of detail_verdict_text (full field) + targeted one-sentence patch to detail_evidence paragraph 3 + tldr_verdict replacement. Propagates the EXP-220 / WIN-010 c5 FSF factual-error correction to WIN-068. Addresses ISS-1398 (FSF empirical-fitting claim inherits WIN-010 c5 factual error). Keeps the triple-count cluster on consistent framing (FSF = H(r)/r is genuine geometric scaling; anchor magnitude is imported from observation; Kim & Chang 2018 as shared standard-physics anchor).
patch_instructions:
Three edits to the WIN with id='068' in data/wins.json:
(1) detail_verdict_text: replace the ENTIRE field with replacement_detail_verdict_text.
(2) detail_evidence: find the unique substring detail_evidence_sentence_patch.before (verified 1 occurrence, 2026-04-22) and replace it with detail_evidence_sentence_patch.after. Only the last sentence of paragraph 3 is touched; paragraphs 1, 2, the rest of paragraph 3, and paragraph 4 are unchanged.
(3) tldr_verdict: replace the ENTIRE field with replacement_tldr_verdict.
All other WIN-068 fields (claim, finding, new_in_v51, detail_claim, detail_extra, detail_group, code_analysis, tldr_evidence, verdict, verdict_history) are unchanged. Note: the verdict ("Std Model Explains") does NOT change — the correction strengthens the non-discrimination argument by removing a factual-error foothold a defender could exploit; it does not alter the verdict.
replacement_detail_verdict_text:
Non-discriminating: The Chapman ionospheric mechanism — first described by Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38:3) and quantitatively confirmed across 39 eclipses by Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054) using INTERMAGNET data — predicts the ensemble signal (Y-component increase, X/Z/F decreases at magnetic latitudes 30°–50° over ~180-minute windows) without any dome parameters. The moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, suppressing E-region conductivity and the Sq current system; the resulting magnetic perturbation tracks the eclipse shadow path with predictable amplitude and timing. This mechanism operates across all 39 eclipses in the dome's ensemble and does not require the dome's cavity geometry, firmament height, or any other disc parameter.

Credit where it is due on the scaling itself: the dome's 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) uses a field-strength factor that genuinely equals H(r)/r normalized to Boulder. Solving the dome's own V12 coordinate equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) for each station's latitude and computing (H(r)/r)/(H(rBOU)/rBOU) reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values (0.642–2.075) to better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst (Pearson r = 0.9999 between predicted and claimed). Our prior framing of FSF as "empirical fitting, not dome-geometric derivation" was quantitatively wrong and has been retracted in the WIN-010/025/068 cluster; the dome's FSF really is dome geometry.

Being right about the scaling law between stations is not the same as predicting the signal. The 39-eclipse ensemble counted here is a retrospective citation of Kim & Chang (2018): FSF does not enter the 1991–2016 data at all — it is only invoked for the 2026 forward projection. For that projection, the absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor (−10.9 nT) is adopted from the 2017 observation, not derived from H(r), n(r), or any other dome parameter; every other station's predicted delta_Z is a 2017 BOU number scaled by a geometric ratio. Standard Sq modeling, by contrast, derives absolute magnitudes at each station from parameters sourced independently — E-region electron density from ionosonde networks, GPS/TEC, and climatological averages — none of which are fitted to the eclipse signal being predicted. The dome reuses the specific 2017 BOU observation as both a counted WIN (WIN-010) and the 2026 baseline, which is in-sample evaluation, not out-of-sample calibration. The scaling shape is also not uniquely dome-geometric: any smooth monotonic H(r) combined with r·tan(θ) = H(r) produces a latitude-dependent ratio of the same form, so matching one parametrization to 0.1% does not discriminate the dome from any alternative disc-geometry model with a comparable height function.

The dome's own Steelman Evidence Ladder places eclipse geomagnetic evidence at Tier 1, explicitly acknowledging it is consistent with both models — an admission that WIN-068 alone cannot distinguish dome cavity dynamics from standard ionospheric physics.

Even granting that FSF = H(r)/r is a valid geometric scaling, Section 1.8 (The Coordinate Collapse) shows the disc-radius values that scaling feeds on are themselves computed from observed Polaris elevation angles, travelling through the same n(r) medium the dome concedes is 300–6,000× more refractive than Earth's atmosphere. Every dome-coordinate r in the FSF table inherits that contamination, so even the one genuine derivation the dome has produced anchors on numbers that are not what the dome thinks they are.

WIN-010 (BOU 2017), WIN-025 (2024 9-station), and WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble 1991–2016) all invoke the same ionospheric Sq mechanism at three different aggregation levels. The dome counts each as a separate confirmed prediction, inflating the score count without adding independent evidence.

Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails the post-hoc test. The confirming data comes from Kim & Chang's published dataset covering 1991–2016, publicly available years before the dome incorporated it (Kim & Chang published in 2018; the dome incorporated it only in V51.0, early 2026 — an eight-year gap). Under the dome author's own timestamping definition, no pre-registration of the expected value in a venue independent of the author could be located. The monitor script has no validation logic for WIN-068 (monitoring = "none"), and the 39-eclipse ensemble is a retrospective literature citation, not a live prediction.

detail_evidence_sentence_patch.before: The dome's FSF scaling factor, by contrast, is calibrated directly against the observed eclipse magnetic data it claims to predict, which is circular.
detail_evidence_sentence_patch.after: The dome's FSF scaling factor, by contrast, genuinely equals H(r)/r normalized to Boulder — direct recomputation reproduces the nine published FSF values at Pearson r = 0.9999, so the geometric derivation the dome claims is real and we retract our prior "empirical fitting" framing. But FSF is a station-to-station ratio only: every predicted delta_Z multiplies the 2017 BOU observation (−10.9 nT) by a geometric factor, so the absolute magnitudes the 2026 forward projection claims to predict anchor on training data. The 39-eclipse ensemble counted here does not invoke FSF at all — it is a retrospective citation of the Kim & Chang (2018) dataset with no dome-derived prediction attached.
detail_evidence_sentence_patch.rationale: Replaces the last sentence of detail_evidence paragraph 3. Propagates the EXP-220 / WIN-010 c5 correction: FSF = H(r)/r is genuinely dome geometry (Pearson r=0.9999), not empirical fitting. Pivots to the ratio-vs-absolute-magnitude argument and notes that WIN-068's ensemble claim is specifically a literature retrospective where FSF doesn't enter.

current_tldr_verdict: The Chapman mechanism (90+ years old) predicts the entire ensemble signal without dome parameters. The dome adds no derivation — only a post-hoc scaling formula calibrated against observed eclipse data. Triple-counting WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068 for a single ionospheric mechanism inflates the score count.
replacement_tldr_verdict: The Chapman mechanism (documented since 1933 and confirmed across 39 eclipses by Kim & Chang 2018) predicts the ensemble signal without any dome parameters. The dome does genuinely derive the station-to-station FSF scaling (FSF = H(r)/r, Pearson r = 0.9999) — we retract our earlier empirical-fitting framing — but the 39-eclipse ensemble counted here is a retrospective Kim & Chang citation that never invokes FSF, and the 2026 forward projection still imports its absolute magnitude from the 2017 Boulder observation. Triple-counting WIN-010/025/068 for one mechanism inflates the score.

holes_addressed.hole: H1 (critical) — ISS-1398 — FSF empirical-fitting factual error propagated from WIN-010 c5
holes_addressed.description: Old WIN-068 detail_verdict_text paragraph 2: "Its formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) is a scaling relation calibrated against observed eclipse data, not a derivation from dome geometry. The FSF (field strength factor) values range 0.642–2.075 across nine European stations — a 3.2× spread among stations at similar disc radii — consistent with empirical fitting, not dome-geometric derivation from H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619)." Also in detail_evidence paragraph 3: "The dome's FSF scaling factor, by contrast, is calibrated directly against the observed eclipse magnetic data it claims to predict, which is circular." Both claims are quantitatively falsified by direct recomputation: FSF = (H(r)/r)/(H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values to better than 0.1% at 7 of 9 stations, <1.8% worst case, Pearson r = 0.9999. The FSF derivation the dome claims is genuine.
holes_addressed.fix: detail_verdict_text paragraph 2 now explicitly credits FSF = H(r)/r as a real geometric derivation with numerical verification (0.1%/1.8%/r=0.9999) and explicitly retracts the empirical-fitting framing. Paragraph 3 pivots to the arguments that hold: (a) scaling between stations is not a prediction of the absolute BOU magnitude, which is imported from the 2017 observation; (b) any smooth H(r) plus the r·tan(θ)=H(r) convention produces the same shape, so the 0.1% match does not uniquely identify dome geometry vs any alternative disc-geometry height-function model; (c) the training-as-test critique is preserved (reframed as in-sample vs out-of-sample evaluation); (d) NEW for WIN-068: the 39-eclipse ensemble counted here is a retrospective citation of Kim & Chang (2018), so FSF doesn't enter the 1991–2016 data at all — only the 2026 forward projection invokes it. detail_evidence paragraph 3 last sentence rewritten to match. The phrase 'at similar disc radii' is deleted (defender can compute r values and show stations span r=3330–7021 km; the claim was factually wrong in WIN-010, same check applies here).
holes_addressed.inverts: Position: was 'FSF is empirical fitting, not derivation'; now 'FSF really is geometric scaling, but scaling is not a magnitude prediction, and for the 39-eclipse ensemble specifically, FSF does not enter the citation at all.' Triple-count cluster (WIN-010/025/068) now uses consistent framing. Advocate-mode defense (defense_survives=5 in the WIN-010 c5 review) collapses here as well.

holes_addressed.hole: H2 (moderate) — triple-count cluster consistency — one retraction applied everywhere
holes_addressed.description: EXP-220 retracted the FSF empirical-fitting claim in WIN-010. Without propagation, WIN-068 and WIN-025 retained contradictory framing, and an adversarial cross-reader greps for 'FSF' across the three panels would catch the inconsistency immediately. The triple-count note itself only works if the three panels share standard-physics framing; factual-error divergence undermines the cluster.
holes_addressed.fix: WIN-068 now explicitly names the retraction ('Our prior framing of FSF as "empirical fitting, not dome-geometric derivation" was quantitatively wrong and has been retracted in the WIN-010/025/068 cluster') so a reader hitting any of the three panels sees a consistent story and an explicit statement that we corrected ourselves.

holes_addressed.hole: H3 (moderate) — Stronger argument for WIN-068: the ensemble does not use FSF
holes_addressed.description: The WIN-010 rewrite (EXP-220) concedes FSF as geometry and pivots to anchor-magnitude critique. The parallel argument is even stronger for WIN-068 because the 39-eclipse ensemble is a retrospective literature citation: Kim & Chang (2018) covers 1991–2016, the dome did not predict any of those 39 eclipses, and FSF is only invoked in the separate 2026 forward projection. So for WIN-068 specifically, the 'no dome-derived prediction' argument holds even without the FSF retraction — the ensemble is counted as a WIN but is purely a literature citation.
holes_addressed.fix: New paragraph 3 in detail_verdict_text and new last sentence of detail_evidence paragraph 3 both make this explicit. This is a genuinely stronger argument than WIN-010 supports (because WIN-010 involves a specific observation that the dome extends with FSF; WIN-068 involves only retrospective data). Framed as a parallel but distinct pivot.

holes_addressed.hole: H4 (minor) — tldr_verdict contained the same factual error
holes_addressed.description: Current tldr_verdict: 'The dome adds no derivation — only a post-hoc scaling formula calibrated against observed eclipse data.' The 'scaling formula calibrated against observed eclipse data' part is the empirical-fitting claim in layman's phrasing and is factually wrong (FSF is H(r)/r geometry). A layman-facing TLDR that contradicts the main text after correction would be worse than the original.
holes_addressed.fix: tldr_verdict replaced. New version concedes the scaling derivation, names Kim & Chang (2018) as the shared anchor, makes the ratio-vs-absolute-magnitude point in plain English, and notes the 39-eclipse ensemble does not invoke FSF. Triple-count line preserved.

new_evidence_added: Pearson r = 0.9999 and <0.1%/<1.8% per-station error figures for FSF = (H(r)/r)/(H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) — same computation documented in monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/WIN-010.c5.json quantitative_verification (cross-verified 2026-04-22, recomputed independently for this EXP)
V12 coordinate equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) named explicitly (was implicit in prior text)
Explicit statement that the 39-eclipse ensemble (Kim & Chang 2018 data, 1991–2016) does not use FSF — new framing not present in prior text

anticipated_objections.objection: You retracted 'FSF is empirical fitting' — doesn't that collapse the whole non-discrimination case for WIN-068?
anticipated_objections.response: No. WIN-068 is counted as a confirmed WIN for the 39-eclipse ensemble (Kim & Chang 2018, 1991–2016). FSF is only invoked in the separate 2026 forward projection. The non-discrimination case for the ensemble itself rests on Chapman (1933) + Kim & Chang (2018) predicting the signal from independent ionospheric parameters (ionosonde, GPS/TEC, climatology) — that case is untouched by the FSF retraction, and is in fact stronger once we remove the factually wrong claim the defender could have used to attack us.

anticipated_objections.objection: If FSF is H(r)/r and reproduces the dome's table at Pearson 0.9999, isn't that a successful dome-derived prediction?
anticipated_objections.response: It is a successful ratio prediction between stations, which is strictly weaker than a magnitude prediction. The 2026 forward projection computes station_i delta_Z = (−10.9 nT at BOU 2017) × shadow_coverage × (H(r_i)/r_i)/(H(r_BOU)/r_BOU). The (−10.9 nT) factor is an observed number; the shadow coverage is standard Besselian geometry; only the last factor is FSF. So the dome predicts nine numbers by multiplying an observation by a geometric ratio. Standard Sq modeling predicts those nine absolute magnitudes from ionospheric electron density without reference to the 2017 BOU value at all. Stronger prediction, stronger theory.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 39-eclipse ensemble is a real signal in real data. Why does it not count as a dome win?
anticipated_objections.response: Because neither the signal nor its latitude-dependence nor its timescale is derived from dome parameters. The dome cites Kim & Chang (2018) — a standard-physics paper that confirms Chapman 1933 — and relabels the Sq mechanism as 'firmament cavity induction.' No dome equation predicts Y-component increase at magnetic latitudes 30°–50° over 180-minute windows; Chapman + modern Sq modeling does. Citing someone else's confirmation of standard physics is not a dome prediction, regardless of how well-documented that confirmation is.

anticipated_objections.objection: If the 2026 eclipse produces delta_Z values matching the dome's FSF-scaled predictions, would that be a dome win?
anticipated_objections.response: It would be a station-to-station ratio-matching success, which is weaker than a magnitude-prediction success. The test that would discriminate: whether 2026 delta_Z tracks FSF (H(r)/r geometry) more tightly than it tracks eclipse shadow coverage (Besselian-elements geometry, the standard-physics prediction). Kim & Chang's ensemble says the response tracks shadow geometry at magnetic latitudes 30°–50°. If FSF beats shadow-coverage on a clean out-of-sample nine-station test, that would be non-trivial. If they match comparably, the dome's scaling is indistinguishable from coverage. The 2026-08-12 eclipse is the clean external test — we will update this WIN when that data arrives (see WIN-010 for the specific per-station predictions EBR −10.7 through COI −10.1 nT).

kernel_of_truth: The dome is genuinely correct that (a) 39-eclipse ensemble geomagnetic signals exist at the amplitudes Kim & Chang (2018) report, and the Chapman Sq mechanism does explain them; (b) FSF = (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) is a clean geometric scaling from dome's V12 coordinate equation that reproduces the dome's published FSF values at Pearson r = 0.9999 — a legitimate derivation from dome parameters. Our prior reviews described FSF as 'empirical fitting' and 'calibrated against the observed eclipse data'; that description was quantitatively wrong and we retract it. What the dome has NOT done is (i) derive the absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor (imported from the 2017 observation), (ii) predict the 1991–2016 ensemble as a forward-looking dome claim (it is a retrospective literature citation), or (iii) show that the scaling shape is uniquely dome-geometric rather than a generic consequence of any smooth H(r) plus the r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention. Standard Sq modeling derives magnitudes directly from independent ionospheric parameters, which is strictly stronger than a ratio + observed anchor.

cross_win_consistency_flag.issue: WIN-025 detail_verdict_text retains the stale 'dome's refraction cascade' + 'sun's physical position seasonally indeterminate' language that was corrected in WIN-010 (EXP-220) via the Section 1.8 cross-reference update (HNOTE-REFRACT-RESTRUCTURE-001, 2026-04-12 — Section 1.8 renamed to 'The Coordinate Collapse'). EXP-222 deliberately scopes only WIN-068 per the tracker note and ISS-1398 ('Do NOT expand EXP-220 scope'). Also, WIN-025's text does not contain the exact 'empirical fitting' phrasing that WIN-010 and WIN-068 had, so the FSF factual error does NOT propagate to WIN-025 as severely — WIN-025 just mentions FSF in a bridging sentence to Section 1.8. Recommend decider triage: does WIN-025 need a small EXP to update the Section 1.8 cross-reference + pick up the FSF = H(r)/r concession for cluster consistency, or is the existing WIN-025 text compatible with the corrected WIN-010/068 framing?
cross_win_consistency_flag.recommended_action: Decider: open a new LOW-PRIORITY issue 'WIN-025 Section 1.8 cross-reference + FSF framing catch-up' and assign a small EXP (or self-patch). Scope: update the 'dome's refraction cascade' / 'sun's physical position seasonally indeterminate' sentence to the current 'Coordinate Collapse / r contaminated by Polaris-angle measurement' framing; either keep FSF silent (WIN-025 does not assert empirical fitting) or add the same retraction acknowledgment for cluster consistency. This is cosmetic cluster alignment, not a factual-error fix — WIN-025 is not directly affected by the FSF empirical-fitting error.

out_of_scope_deliberately: WIN-025 Section 1.8 cross-reference + cluster framing — flagged for decider (see cross_win_consistency_flag)

WIN-068 detail_evidence paragraph 2 ('physical sun altitude indeterminate' / refraction cascade-era framing) — this paragraph makes a separate cavity-mechanism argument and is not the subject of ISS-1398. A future EXP may harmonize it with the current Section 1.8 framing. Out of scope for EXP-222 per tracker note.

Any code_analysis tag changes — derives_from_dome remains false (FSF scaling is derivative of dome geometry as a ratio, but the WIN-068 ensemble claim itself is not a dome-derived prediction). post_hoc and relabels_standard tags unchanged.
propagation_note: EXP-220 rewrote WIN-010 for the same factual error (ISS-1390). EXP-222 is the parallel WIN-068 rewrite per ISS-1398 and the EXP-220 cross_win_consistency_flag. After both land, WIN-010 and WIN-068 share consistent framing: FSF = H(r)/r is genuine geometric scaling (conceded); Kim & Chang (2018) is the shared standard-physics anchor; triple-count note references all three WINs; 2026-08-12 eclipse is the clean external test.
